name: Italian sweet red pepper (food medium)
description: Food-derived medium: metabolizable components of Italian sweet red pepper (Capsicum annuum) [Vegetables] from FooDB, plus a standard M9 mineral base. Organic components use presence-based uptake bounds; FooDB content retained. Measured compounds with no BiGG exchange are listed under uncovered.

provenance.citation: FooDB v2020-04-07 (Wishart DS et al., foodb.ca). Content sources: All-Creatures, DTU, MANUAL, PHENOL EXPLORER, USDA.
provenance.notes: Organic components mapped from FooDB compounds to BiGG via InChIKey/name. Mineral base added. Bounds presence-based; foodb_content retained. Unmapped measured compounds kept in uncovered (v2 completeness fix). IMPORTANT: composition values are taken from the archived FooDB 2020-04-07 CSV data release; FooDB's live food page no longer displays this bulk composition, so it will appear empty. Verify individual compounds via the per-component cross-reference links. Underlying content was aggregated by FooDB from USDA / DTU / Phenol Explorer.
provenance.verification: expert-curated (multi-source food composition; growth-relevant profile completed, FooDB noise removed)
curation_sources: USDA FoodData Central FDC 170108 (Peppers, sweet, red, raw; SR Legacy)
USDA FoodData Central FDC 2345321 (Pepper, sweet, red, raw; Foundation Foods; individual sugars)
Comprehensive Capsicum annuum fruit-quality / organic-acid assessment, PMC9871545 (citric, malic, fumaric, 2-oxoglutarate, oxalic ranges)
Sugars and ripening in Capsicum, Plants 2023, doi:10.3390/plants12020231 (PMC9863480)
Biochemical characterization of traditional Italian (Campania) sweet peppers, PMC7346132 (ascorbic acid, phenolics in red varieties)
FooDB FOOD00881 (base record, betaine, boron, pigment presence)
provenance.curation_notes: Ripe red sweet (bell) Capsicum annuum. Dominant carbon/energy sources for a microbe: the free hexoses D-fructose (~2.3 g) and D-glucose (~1.9 g) plus citric acid (~0.35 g); sucrose is essentially gone at full ripeness. Organic acids (citric >> malic, fumaric, 2-oxoglutarate, oxalic) were entirely missing from the FooDB profile and are added here; their absolute values carry more uncertainty than the USDA panel because organic-acid quantities derive from Capsicum HPLC ranges (PMC9871545) rather t
curation_added: citric acid (dominant organic acid, was absent)
D-fructose and D-glucose with real amounts (fructose > glucose; sucrose ~0 at full ripeness)
full USDA amino-acid profile with amounts

curation_removed.name: Arsenate
curation_removed.reason: Non-nutritive toxic trace element; not a growth nutrient

curation_removed.name: Rubidium
curation_removed.reason: Non-nutritive trace element (K analogue), not a growth nutrient

curation_removed.name: Vanadium
curation_removed.reason: Non-nutritive trace element at food matrix

curation_removed.name: Co2+ / Cobalt
curation_removed.reason: Sub-trace; no vitamin B12 in a plant food so no metabolic role here; FooDB noise

curation_removed.name: Nickel
curation_removed.reason: Sub-trace contaminant-level; not a defined growth nutrient of this food

components.name: quinic acid
components.evidence_source: Capsicum pericarp phytochemistry (81.3 mg/100g DW, ~8% dry matter)

components.name: L-ascorbate
components.evidence_source: USDA FDC 170108; Italian Campania sweet pepper PMC7346132 (0.82-1.20 mg/g in red varieties)
